To find the number of practice sessions that maximizes the average batting score, we analyze the quadratic equation \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). This is a downward-opening parabola, so its maximum occurs at the vertex. The vertex formula for \( n \) in \( an^2 + bn + c \) is \( n = -b / (2a) \). Here, \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Calculating the value gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) = -5 / -0.4 = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions are typically whole numbers, we check integers 12 and 13. For \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). For \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both yield the same maximum score of 81.2. Thus, the optimal number of sessions is 12.5, and the maximum average batting score is 81.2.

Next, we calculate the change in win probability when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, find \( S \) for \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 0.2(25) = 70 \). Then find \( S \) for \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 0.2(100) = 80 \). Now apply the logistic function \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \). For \( S = 70 \), the exponent is \( -(0.1 \times 70 - 3) = -(7 - 3) = -4 \). So \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 1 / (1 + 0.0183) \approx 0.982 \).
